Based on the search results, here's an overview of how rich text editors work internally and the architecture of major frameworks:

## Core Architecture Patterns

Rich text editors like ProseMirror bridge the gap between editing explicit content like Markdown or XML and classical WYSIWYG editors by implementing a WYSIWYG-style editing interface for documents more constrained and structured than plain HTML.

## Major Frameworks and Their Approaches

**ProseMirror:** ProseMirror is a toolkit for building rich text editors with a focus on modularity and flexibility, and is highly customizable, allowing developers to define their own schema.(no content)It has built-in, ground-up support for collaborative editing, where multiple people work on the same document in real time.

**Slate:** Slate can be thought of like a pluggable implementation of contenteditable built on top of React.(no content)With a plugin-first architecture and a schema-less core, it becomes clearer where the boundary is between "core" and "custom".(no content)Slate's data model closely mirrors the Document Object Model (DOM), using a nested tree structure for representing documents and supporting standard DOM concepts like selections and ranges.

**Tiptap:** Tiptap is built on top of ProseMirror, and abstracts away the cumbersome parts of ProseMirror, leaving an enjoyable developer experience.

## Decorations and Visual Features

Slate (Plate) and ProseMirror (Tiptap, Remirror, BlockNote) both have a pure decorations feature. This allows styling content without modifying the document structure itself.

## 2025 Landscape

Tiptap, Lexical, ProseMirror, Slate, and Plate are all headless frameworks offering low-level primitives for developers to build fully customized UIs. The choice between them typically depends on your framework preference, customization needs, and collaboration requirements.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about contenteditable, custom elements, rendering, and block editor architecture:

## ContentEditable Fundamentals

The contenteditable global attribute is an enumerated attribute indicating if the element should be editable by the user, and if so, the browser modifies its widget to allow editing.(no content)The contenteditable attribute is an enumerated attribute whose keywords are the empty string (""), "events", "caret", "typing", "plaintext-only", "true", and "false".

## Block Editor Architecture

Unlike traditional WYSIWYG editors that rely on a single contenteditable HTML element, a block editor breaks down content into discrete units—blocks—that can be individually edited, moved, and styled.(no content)Each block can be seen as a React component, encapsulating its logic, presentation, and data, and this modularity enhances the user experience and simplifies the developer's task in creating and maintaining complex editing environments.

## Custom Elements and Rendering

Converters inform the editor how to convert the view to the model (for example, when loading data to the editor or handling pasted content) and how to render the model to the view (for editing purposes, or when retrieving the editor data).(no content)Passing the element through toWidget() will make its content non-editable, conversely, passing it through toWidgetEditable() will make its content editable again.

## Rendering Challenges

Typically rendering to contenteditable elements is tough because they edit the DOM directly, causing most rendering libraries, like React and Preact to be confused, since their underlying Virtual DOMs don't match up with the actual DOM structure anymore.

The search results provide practical information about how modern block editors (like those in WordPress/Gutenberg and CKEditor 5) use component-based architectures alongside contenteditable attributes to create flexible editing experiences.

Links: [{"title":"ProseMirror Guide","url":"https://prosemirror.net/docs/guide/"},{"title":"ProseMirror Reference manual","url":"https://prosemirror.net/docs/ref/"},{"title":"Review: ProseMirror Basics","url":"https://nytimes.github.io/oak-byo-react-prosemirror-redux/post/prosemirror-basics/"},{"title":"GitHub - PierBover/prosemirror-cookbook: A series of examples for understanding ProseMirror","url":"https://github.com/PierBover/prosemirror-cookbook"},{"title":"Tiptap Concepts | Tiptap Editor Docs","url":"https://tiptap.dev/docs/editor/core-concepts/introduction"},{"title":"Can't update a state with a transaction when the initial state does not match a schema · Issue #840 · ProseMirror/prosemirror","url":"https://github.com/ProseMirror/prosemirror/issues/840"},{"title":"How to use the prosemirror-model.Schema function in prosemirror-model | Snyk","url":"https://snyk.io/advisor/npm-package/prosemirror-model/functions/prosemirror-model.Schema"},{"title":"ProseMirror schema example","url":"https://prosemirror.net/examples/schema/"},{"title":"prosemirror-state/src/README.md at master · ProseMirror/prosemirror-state","url":"https://github.com/ProseMirror/prosemirror-state/blob/master/src/README.md"},{"title":"ProseMirror 1.0","url":"https://marijnhaverbeke.nl/blog/prosemirror-1.html"}]

## ProseMirror Document Model, Schema, Transactions, and State Management

Here's a comprehensive overview of these core ProseMirror concepts:

### Document Model

ProseMirror's document model is the data structure used to describe the content of the editor, and ProseMirror maintains a tree of elements called "nodes" which are directly stored in state as view.state.doc.

### Schema

ProseMirror's schema feature means the core editor has no built-in opinion about what a document looks like, and instead looks at configurable data (the schema) to figure out what kind of content is allowed and how it is structured. A node spec specifies what props a node takes (called "attributes"), what type of children it allows (called "content"), and how it should be represented in the DOM (via toDOM).

The schema is used to create a state, which will generate an empty document conforming to the schema, and a default selection at the start of that document.

### State Management

ProseMirror-state provides the data structure that describes the editor's whole state, including the selection, and a transaction system for moving from one state to the next.

### Transactions

When the user types or otherwise interacts with the view, it generates 'state transactions'. This means it does not just modify the document in-place, but instead every change causes a transaction to be created, which describes the changes that are made to the state, and can be applied to create a new state.

Transactions track changes to the document and other state changes like selection updates and adjustments of stored marks. You can also store metadata properties in a transaction, which are extra pieces of information that client code or plugins can use to describe what a transaction represents.

Links: [{"title":"Exploring Notion's Data Model: A Block-Based Architecture | Notion","url":"https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion"},{"title":"Examining Notion's Backend Architecture | Relbis Labs","url":"https://labs.relbis.com/blog/2024-04-18_notion_backend/"},{"title":"Build a Notion-like editor with Rails | Rails Designer","url":"https://railsdesigner.com/rails-block-editor/"},{"title":"How the Notion Editor Works","url":"https://yuexun.me/how-the-notion-editor-works/"},{"title":"Notion's Journey Through Different Stages of Data Scale","url":"https://www.onehouse.ai/blog/notions-journey-through-different-stages-of-data-scale"},{"title":"Stephen Ou - The Beauty of Notion","url":"https://stephenou.com/beauty-of-notion"},{"title":"Examining Notion’s Backend Architecture | by Michael Lapin | Relbis Labs | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/relbis-labs/examining-notions-backend-architecture-4c708d8f9b83"},{"title":"Storing 200 Billion Entities: Notion’s Data Lake Project","url":"https://blog.bytebytego.com/p/storing-200-billion-entities-notions"},{"title":"Notion System Design Explained","url":"https://www.educative.io/blog/notion-system-design"},{"title":"Exploring Notion: Understanding and Utilizing Blocks | StoryFlint","url":"https://www.storyflint.com/dives/notion-blocks"}]

I've found comprehensive information about Notion's editor architecture, document model, blocks, and rendering system. Here are the key findings:

## Block-Based Architecture

Notion blocks are the singular pieces that represent all units of information inside the Notion editor, and the attributes of a block determine how that information is rendered and organized.(no content)Everything you see in the Notion editor (texts, images, headings, lists, pages, etc) is modeled as a "block" entity in the backend, the block types may have different front-end representations and behaviors, but they are all stored in a Postgres database with a consistent structure, schema, and associated metadata.

## Block Components

Each block is uniquely identifiable by its ID, and Notion uses randomly-generated UUIDs (UUID v4) for IDs.(no content)Properties form a data structure containing custom attributes about a specific block, with the most common property being title, which stores the text content of block types like paragraphs, lists.(no content)Every block has a type, which defines how a block is displayed, and how the block's properties are interpreted.

## Structural Organization

In Notion, indentation is structural: it's a reflection of the structure of the render tree—when you indent something in Notion, you are manipulating relationships between blocks and their content, not just adding a style.

## Rendering Process

All data loaded by loadPageChunk is put into memory, and once the data is in memory, the page is laid out and rendered using React.(no content)React renders the view, but Notion implements its own render queue—every edit creates a transaction with operations that modify block data.(no content)Text formatting is stored as structured data, not HTML, and the editor rebuilds DOM from data.

## Data Persistence

The transaction is saved into TransactionQueue, the part of the client responsible for sending all transactions to Notion's servers so your data is persisted and shared with collaborators, and TransactionQueue stores transactions safely in IndexedDB or SQLite until they're persisted by the server or rejected.

content.thinking: Excelente! Agora tenho informações completas sobre como editores como o Notion funcionam. Vou compilar uma resposta abrangente em português, como solicitado nas instruções do projeto.

content.text:
## Como Editores Rich Text (Notion, etc.) Funcionam Internamente

A arquitetura desses editores é baseada em alguns conceitos fundamentais:

### 1. Modelo de Dados Baseado em Blocos

No Notion, **tudo é um bloco** - textos, imagens, headings, listas, páginas. Cada bloco possui:

```
Block {
  id: UUID v4           // identificador único
  type: string          // "paragraph", "heading", "image", etc.
  properties: {}        // atributos (ex: title contém o texto)
  parent_id: UUID       // bloco pai (hierarquia)
  children: UUID[]      // blocos filhos
}
```

A indentação é **estrutural**, não estilística - indentar manipula relacionamentos entre blocos na árvore.

### 2. Schema e Documento Estruturado

Frameworks como **ProseMirror** usam um schema que define:
- Quais tipos de nós são permitidos
- Que filhos cada nó pode ter
- Como serializar/deserializar para DOM

```typescript
// Exemplo simplificado de schema
const schema = new Schema({
  nodes: {
    doc: { content: "block+" },
    paragraph: { content: "inline*", toDOM: () => ["p", 0] },
    heading: { attrs: { level: 1 }, content: "inline*" }
  },
  marks: {
    bold: { toDOM: () => ["strong", 0] }
  }
})
```

### 3. Transactions e Estado Imutável

O padrão é **nunca modificar o documento diretamente**. Cada edição cria uma **transaction** que descreve as mudanças:

```
Estado A → Transaction → Estado B (novo)
```

Isso permite:
- **Undo/Redo** trivial
- **Colaboração em tempo real** (CRDTs/OT)
- **Sincronização** via fila de transactions

No Notion, transactions são salvas em `IndexedDB`/`SQLite` localmente até serem confirmadas pelo servidor.

### 4. Renderização com React + ContentEditable

O fluxo típico:

```
Dados (Blocos)
    ↓
React Virtual DOM
    ↓
DOM com contenteditable
    ↓
Eventos do usuário (input, keydown)
    ↓
Intercepta → Cria Transaction → Novo Estado
    ↓
Re-renderiza React
```

O desafio é que `contenteditable` modifica o DOM diretamente, causando dessincronia com o Virtual DOM. Soluções:

- **ProseMirror**: Reconcilia mudanças do DOM com seu modelo interno
- **Slate**: Trata o `contenteditable` como input controlado
- **Notion**: Implementa sua própria fila de renderização

### 5. Decorações (Sem Modificar Dados)

Para features visuais como:
- Syntax highlighting
- Seleção colaborativa (cursores de outros usuários)
- Placeholders

Usa-se **decorations** - overlays visuais que não alteram o documento.

### 6. Arquitetura de Plugins

Editores modernos são **headless** e extensíveis:

| Framework | Abordagem |
|-----------|-----------|
| ProseMirror | Modular, schema-first, plugins |
| Slate | Schema-less, plugin-first, React-native |
| Tiptap | Abstração sobre ProseMirror |
| Lexical (Meta) | Estado atômico, plugins |

### Exemplo: Bloco de Código

Quando você digita ` ``` ` no Notion:

1. **Input Listener** detecta o padrão
2. Cria **Transaction**: `replaceBlock(paragraph → code_block)`
3. **Schema** valida a transformação
4. **React** re-renderiza com componente `<CodeBlock>`
5. O componente pode ter seu próprio editor interno (ex: CodeMirror)

---

**Sources:**
- [The Data Model Behind Notion](https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion)
- [How the Notion Editor Works](https://yuexun.me/how-the-notion-editor-works/)
- [ProseMirror Guide](https://prosemirror.net/docs/guide/)
- [Slate GitHub](https://github.com/ianstormtaylor/slate)
- [Rich Text Editor Frameworks 2025](https://liveblocks.io/blog/which-rich-text-editor-framework-should-you-choose-in-2025)
- [BlockNote - Notion-style Editor](https://github.com/TypeCellOS/BlockNote)

I've performed the web search and found comprehensive information about rich text editors, document trees, and AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) implementations.

## Key Findings:

**Rich Text Editors Using AST:**

When you save content in a rich text editor, it is saved as an abstract syntax tree (AST), and can be queried in HTML, Markdown, Text, and raw AST formats (JSON). The AST is based on Slate. Additionally, the underlying storage format of Rich Text fields is a JSON Abstract Syntax Tree, which is based primarily on the Lexical.dev library.

**Document Tree Structure:**

A Blockquote represents a section quoted from somewhere else, and Flow content represents the sections of a document.(no content)Container nodes can contain child nodes representing elements with nested content (like paragraphs and lists), while Leaf nodes cannot contain children and represent atomic elements (like text and images), creating a clean distinction between structural and content elements.

**AST Fundamentals:**

An AST does not include nonessential punctuation and delimiters (braces, semicolons, parentheses, etc.).(no content)An AST is a tree data structure representing any structured text file, and since it's "abstract," it does not have a standard representation because every language may have its own specific dictionary.

**Bi-directional Conversion:**

MDXEditor implements a bi-directional conversion between the Markdown Abstract Syntax Tree (MDAST) and the Lexical AST by traversing each tree through a set of visitors that convert the nodes of one tree into the other.

Links: [{"title":"Nodes | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/concepts/02-nodes"},{"title":"Data Model | v0.47 | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/v0.47/guides/data-model"},{"title":"Normalizing | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/concepts/11-normalizing"},{"title":"Slatejs + Reactjs, implement full feature editor | by Ngoc Phan | Medium","url":"https://phann123.medium.com/slatejs-reactjs-implement-full-feature-editor-8ed5c2784911"},{"title":"Data Model | TheSunny","url":"https://thesunny.gitbook.io/slate/doc-dev-guides/guides/data-model"},{"title":"Introduction | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/"},{"title":"slate/02-nodes.md at main · ianstormtaylor/slate","url":"https://github.com/ianstormtaylor/slate/blob/main/docs/concepts/02-nodes.md"},{"title":"Block | v0.47 | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/v0.47/slate-core/block"},{"title":"FAQ | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/v/v0.47/general/faq"},{"title":"Nesting Block Elements · Issue #5358 · ianstormtaylor/slate","url":"https://github.com/ianstormtaylor/slate/issues/5358"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about the Slate editor's data structure for nested blocks and inline elements:

## Data Structure Overview

Slate's document model is a nested and recursive tree structure, just like the DOM itself.(no content)Elements can have children nodes—all of which may have children nodes without limit, enabling you to model simple behaviors such as user mentions and hashtags or complex behaviors such as tables and figures with captions.

## Blocks vs. Inlines

All elements default to being "block" elements that appear separated by vertical space and never run into each other.(no content)For certain cases like links, you might make them "inline" flowing elements instead, where they live at the same level as text nodes and flow.(no content)Elements can either contain block elements or inline elements intermingled with text nodes as children, but elements cannot contain some children that are blocks and some that are inlined.

## Core Node Types

Elements make up the middle layers of a rich text document and are the nodes that are custom to your domain.(no content)Text nodes are the lowest-level nodes in the tree, containing the text content along with any formatting, which is how you implement custom formatting like bold, italic, and code.

## Customization

You can define which nodes are treated as inline nodes by overriding the editor.isInline function.(no content)Block nodes can only contain other blocks, or inline and text nodes—for example, a paragraph block cannot have another paragraph block element and a link inline element as children at the same time.

Links: [{"title":"Discussion: What are marks - discuss.ProseMirror","url":"https://discuss.prosemirror.net/t/discussion-what-are-marks/862"},{"title":"Discussion: Inline nodes with content - Show - discuss.ProseMirror","url":"https://discuss.prosemirror.net/t/discussion-inline-nodes-with-content/496"},{"title":"ProseMirror Guide","url":"https://prosemirror.net/docs/guide/"},{"title":"website/markdown/guide/doc.md at master · ProseMirror/website","url":"https://github.com/ProseMirror/website/blob/master/markdown/guide/doc.md"},{"title":"ProseMirror Reference manual","url":"https://prosemirror.net/docs/ref/"},{"title":"How to set marks for both block leaf nodes and inline text nodes? - discuss.ProseMirror","url":"https://discuss.prosemirror.net/t/how-to-set-marks-for-both-block-leaf-nodes-and-inline-text-nodes/1012"},{"title":"ProseMirror schema example","url":"https://prosemirror.net/examples/schema/"},{"title":"Document Model | ProseMirror/prosemirror | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/ProseMirror/prosemirror/3.1-document-model"},{"title":"Writer marks/nodes | Kirby CMS","url":"https://getkirby.com/docs/reference/plugins/extensions/writer-marks-nodes"},{"title":"Danger: marks on block nodes - discuss.ProseMirror","url":"https://discuss.prosemirror.net/t/danger-marks-on-block-nodes/1310"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of ProseMirror's node tree structure and the distinction between marks, inline nodes, and block nodes:

## Document Structure

A ProseMirror document is a tree of block nodes, with most of the leaf nodes being textblocks, which are block nodes that contain text.(no content)In ProseMirror, the inline content is modeled as a flat sequence, with the markup attached as metadata to the nodes.

## Block vs. Inline Nodes

ProseMirror's document model mixes flat and tree-shaped content—stretches of inline content (text + elements that appear in between it) are treated as flat, whereas the 'block' structure of the document forms a tree.(no content)Text, hard breaks, and inline images are inline leaf nodes, and a horizontal rule node would be an example of a block leaf node.(no content)At the inline level, each block contains a flat sequence of inline nodes and text.

## Marks

Marks are used to add extra styling or other information to inline content.(no content)Unlike nodes, marks don't affect document structure but add semantic meaning or styling.(no content)Inline nodes hold a set of active marks—things like emphasis or being a link—which are represented as an array of Mark instances.(no content)Adjacent text nodes with the same set of marks are always combined together, and empty text nodes are not allowed. The order in which marks appear is specified by the schema.

## Node Immutability

In ProseMirror, nodes are simply values, and should be approached much as you'd approach the value representing the number 3. 3 can appear in multiple data structures at the same time, it does not have a parent-link to the data structure it is currently part of, and if you add 1 to it, you get a new value, 4, without changing anything about the original 3.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Building real-time collaboration applications: OT vs CRDT","url":"https://www.tiny.cloud/blog/real-time-collaboration-ot-vs-crdt/"},{"title":"Building Collaborative Editing: The Battle Between Operational Transform and CRDTs | by Sohail Saifi | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@sohail_saifi/building-collaborative-editing-the-battle-between-operational-transform-and-crdts-fdceb63c54ac"},{"title":"Real Differences between OT and CRDT under a General Transformation Framework for Consistency Maintenance in Co-Editors | Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction","url":"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3375186"},{"title":"Conflict resolution using OT and CRDT algorithms","url":"https://www.nitinkumargove.com/blog/conflict-resolution-using-ot-crdt"},{"title":"CRDTs vs. Operational Transformation: How Google Docs Handles Collaborative Editing","url":"https://systemdr.substack.com/p/crdts-vs-operational-transformation"},{"title":"\"CRDTs and OT: The Art of Conflict-Free Data Synchronization\"","url":"https://gaurav789.hashnode.dev/mastering-distributed-collaboration-the-crdt-and-ot-handbook"},{"title":"Eric Winnington - Learning concepts from chatGPT - Operational Transform and Conflict-free Replicated Data Types","url":"https://ewinnington.github.io/posts/learn-from-chatgpt-Crdt-OT"},{"title":"Real Differences between OT and CRDT in Correctness and Complexity for Consistency Maintenance in Co-Editors | Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction","url":"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3392825"},{"title":"Deciding between CRDTs and OT for data synchronization - Tom's Site","url":"https://thom.ee/blog/crdt-vs-operational-transformation/"},{"title":"Real Differences between OT and CRDT in Building Co-","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1905.01517"}]

I've completed the web search for your query about CRDT, OT, operational transformation, collaborative editing, and conflict resolution. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Overview

Two approaches dominate collaborative editing: Operational Transform (OT) and Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs).

## Operational Transformation (OT)

OT relies on an active server connection to coordinate and guarantee all clients operate correctly.(no content)OT treats editing as a sequence of operations that must be transformed based on context. When you type "hello" at position 5, but someone else deleted characters 2-4, your operation needs adjustment—OT transforms your operation: "insert at position 5" becomes "insert at position 3" because two characters vanished.(no content)In case of tie-breaks, OT uses either timestamp, order or priority to resolve which operation should be applied first.

## Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDT)

A CRDT is a data structure that can be replicated across multiple nodes in a distributed system, where each replica can be updated independently and concurrently without coordination between replicas, and all replicas will eventually converge to the same state.(no content)CRDTs take a radically different approach: make the data structure itself handle conflicts. Instead of transforming operations, CRDTs embed enough metadata in the data to make all replicas converge automatically.

## Key Differences

The core distinction: OT centralizes coordination logic in transformation functions. CRDTs distribute it into the data structure itself.

## Real-World Adoption

Interestingly, Google Docs uses OT. Figma switched from OT to CRDTs.(no content)OT remains the choice for building the vast majority of today's co-editors.

I've completed the web search for your query. Here's a summary of what I found:

## Key Results on ContentEditable, Cursor, Selection, and Range APIs for Rich Text Editors

The `window.getSelection()` method returns a Selection object representing the range of text selected by the user or the current position of the caret.(no content)The Range object represents a fragment of a document and holds information about the start and end points of a selection, allowing you to manipulate portions of the document.

### Core Challenges and Solutions

ContentEditable elements use the Selection API, not simpler input methods like `setSelectionRange()`, and Range objects are needed to precisely control cursor placement.(no content)To put the cursor at the end of a contenteditable div, you can use: `document.createRange()`, then `range.selectNodeContents()`, collapse the range, and add it back to the selection.

### Building Rich Text Editors

By using `window.getSelection()` and Range API, you can manipulate text based on user actions and wrap selected text in appropriate HTML tags or apply inline styles.(no content)Contenteditable is typically used with the `execCommand` function, which is very simple to use with most formatting options requiring only a single line of code, and is supported by every browser.

### Modern Approaches

The most reliable way to get the caret position in a contenteditable element is to use the Selection and Range APIs, which are well supported in modern browsers.(no content)In 2026, the best approach is to build on the browser-native Selection and Range APIs, with libraries like caret-pos for serious rich-text interfaces such as mentions, inline formatting, and WYSIWYG editors.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about ProseMirror's document structure compared to AST tree data structures:

## ProseMirror Document Structure

A ProseMirror document is a tree where at each level, a node describes the type of the content, and holds a fragment containing its children.

### Key Differences from Traditional ASTs

**Immutable Values**: In ProseMirror, nodes are simply values that don't change, but can be used as a starting value to compute a modified piece of document. This is fundamentally different from mutable DOM nodes.

**Hybrid Structure**: Rather than using a pure tree structure throughout, the outer part deals with paragraphs, headings, lists in a DOM-like structure with nodes and child nodes, while the content of paragraph nodes is represented as a flat sequence of inline elements, each with associated styles.

**Position Representation**: A position in the document is represented by a path (a sequence of integers denoting child indices at each level) and an offset into the node at the end of this path, which is how cursor positions and modification points are stored.

**Semantic Model**: ProseMirror's tree-shaped data structure describes the structure of text in terms of paragraphs, headings, lists, emphasis, links, and so on.

The document model is explicitly designed around a schema system that provides something like a syntax for documents by setting down which structures are valid.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information comparing these document model approaches in rich text editors:

## Tree-Based Model Structure

Rich text editors use a model tree—a custom data structure which keeps document data and is simplified compared to the DOM or the output HTML. The editor data model is a tree structure with parent elements that may have children nodes (usually text), where characters are organized into text nodes, with each text node grouping all consecutive characters that have the same set of attributes (e.g. formatting), and all text nodes inside their parent element are stored in an array.

## Linear Buffer Representation

Text documents can be accessed in two complementary ways: as a linear buffer for editors to use, and as an object hierarchy useful to layout engines, with the linear representation used for editing and manipulation of the document's contents.

## Comparative Context

Using an array to store and manipulate text is problematic because the entire file must be loaded into the array first, and every insertion and deletion requires each element in the array to be moved. More sophisticated approaches like rope data structures—tree-like structures with leaf nodes containing character buffers—provide the ability to edit strings relatively quickly in logarithmic time.

The search results show that rich text editors typically favor tree-based hierarchical models over flat array structures for better performance and structural representation, though they may use linear buffers for specific editing operations.

(Currently in beta.)","url":"https://github.com/ianstormtaylor/slate"},{"title":"Slate data model: decouple structure and UI aspects. · Issue #2552 · ianstormtaylor/slate","url":"https://github.com/ianstormtaylor/slate/issues/2552"},{"title":"Nodes | Slate","url":"https://docs.slatejs.org/concepts/02-nodes"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Slate editor's data model approach:

## Slate's Nested Tree Structure

Slate uses a nested, recursive tree document model, just like the DOM itself.(no content)This means that creating complex components like tables or nested block quotes are possible for advanced use cases.

## Why Not Flat Structure?

Building complex, nested documents was impossible with many editors that were designed around simplistic "flat" documents, making things like tables, embeds and captions difficult to reason about and sometimes impossible.

## Data Model Constraints

Slate ensures that your editor's content is always of a certain shape through "normalizing". The nested tree model comes with specific structural constraints: block nodes can only contain other blocks, or inline and text nodes, for example, a paragraph block cannot have another paragraph block element and a link inline element as children at the same time.

## Flexibility

It's also easy to keep it simple by only using a single level of hierarchy. So while Slate uses a nested tree structure by design, you can choose to use minimal nesting if your use case doesn't require it.

The nested tree approach in Slate prioritizes the ability to handle complex document structures over the simplicity of a flat, normalized data model.

Here are the search results about ProseMirror's document tree structure, nodes, marks, and internal representation:

## Document Structure Overview

A ProseMirror document is a node, which holds a fragment containing zero or more child nodes. This is a lot like the browser DOM, in that it is recursive and tree-shaped. However, it differs from the DOM in the way it stores inline content. In ProseMirror, the inline content is modeled as a flat sequence, with the markup attached as metadata to the nodes.

## Nodes and Block Structure

A ProseMirror document is a tree of block nodes, with most of the leaf nodes being textblocks, which are block nodes that contain text.(no content)Each node is represented by an instance of the Node class. It is tagged with a type, which knows the node's name, the attributes that are valid for it, and so on.(no content)The content of a node is stored in an instance of Fragment, which holds a sequence of nodes. Even for nodes that don't have or don't allow content, this field is filled (with the shared empty fragment).

## Marks and Inline Content

Inline nodes hold a set of active marks—things like emphasis or being a link—which are represented as an array of Mark instances.(no content)Marks are annotations that apply to ranges of inline content. Unlike nodes, marks don't affect document structure but add semantic meaning or styling.

## Document Invariants

Adjacent text nodes with the same set of marks are always combined together, and empty text nodes are not allowed. The order in which marks appear is specified by the schema.

## Immutable Value Semantics

Nodes are simply values, and should be approached much as you'd approach the value representing the number 3. They don't change, but can be used as a starting value to compute a modified piece of document. They don't know what data structures they are part of, but can be part of multiple structures, or even occur multiple times in a single structure.

Based on the search results, here's information about Lexical's editor data model and architecture:

## Data Model Architecture

An Editor State is the underlying data model that represents what you want to show on the DOM.(no content)Lexical has its own DOM reconciler that takes a set of Editor States (always the "current" and the "pending") and applies a "diff" on them. It then uses this diff to update only the parts of the DOM that need changing.

## Node System

In Lexical, everything you see in the editor — text, paragraphs, images, even custom widgets — is built from nodes. Think of nodes as tiny building blocks. These blocks are arranged in a tree-like structure (kind of like the DOM).

The framework supports different node types:
- TextNode: represents plain or styled text.
- ElementNodes are nodes that can contain other nodes — either TextNodes or even other ElementNodes.(no content)Nodes are created using factory functions with the $create prefix (e.g., $createParagraphNode(), $createTextNode(text)). These functions can only be called within editor.update() or editor.read() contexts.

## State Management Approach

Lexical treats editor states as immutable objects, ensuring stability and predictability in text editing.(no content)The editor state's node tree is recursively frozen after reconciliation to support efficient time travel (undo/redo and similar use cases). Methods that update nodes first call node.getWritable(), which will create a writable clone of a frozen node.

## Meta/Facebook Integration

At Meta, Lexical powers web text editing experiences for hundreds of millions of users everyday across Facebook, Workplace, Messenger, WhatsApp and Instagram.

I've completed the web search on "CRDT text editor Yjs Automerge document structure collaborative". Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Overview

Yjs is a high-performance CRDT for building collaborative applications that sync automatically, exposing its internal CRDT model as shared data types that can be manipulated concurrently.(no content)Automerge is a CRDT implementation with a JSON data model.

## Document Structure & Shared Types

Shared types are common data types like Map or Array with superpowers: changes are automatically distributed to other peers and merged without merge conflicts.(no content)The ytext object is a shared data structure for representing text and also supports formatting attributes (i.e. bold and italic).(no content)Automerge provides a CRDT that can store a JSON object with nested maps and lists.

## Key Features

Yjs supports many existing rich text editors, offline editing, version snapshots, undo/redo and shared cursors, scales well with an unlimited number of users and is well suited for even large documents.(no content)Automerge provides abstractions for capturing changes to JSON documents (not just strings!) and encoding them in a compact binary format that can be applied on other clients.

## Real-World Applications

Yjs is used by several apps in production including JupyterLab and Serenity Notes, an end-to-end encrypted collaborative notes app.(no content)Several collaborative applications have been built using the Automerge CRDT library including PushPin, a digital workspace for media and notes.

Links: [{"title":"Text showdown: Gap Buffers vs Ropes • Core Dumped","url":"https://coredumped.dev/2023/08/09/text-showdown-gap-buffers-vs-ropes/"},{"title":"Text Editor: Data Structures | averylaird.com","url":"https://www.averylaird.com/programming/the%20text%20editor/2017/09/30/the-piece-table"},{"title":"Rope & SumTree — Zed's Blog","url":"https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-rope-sumtree"},{"title":"Text Editor Data Structures - invoke::thought()","url":"https://cdacamar.github.io/data%20structures/algorithms/benchmarking/text%20editors/c++/editor-data-structures/"},{"title":"Efficient Broad-Scale Text Editing and Analysis — In depth Modern Text Editing | by Seth Potter | Medium","url":"https://sapotter.medium.com/efficient-broad-scale-text-editing-and-analysis-in-depth-modern-text-editing-2c3b8ea01b60"},{"title":"Rope (data structure) - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rope_(data_structure)"},{"title":"Gap Buffers, or, Don't Get Tied Up With Ropes? | ScienceBlogs","url":"https://scienceblogs.com/goodmath/2009/02/18/gap-buffers-or-why-bother-with-1"},{"title":"There exists a phenomenal paper comparing ropes, gap buffers, and piece chains I... | Hacker News","url":"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=13102581"},{"title":"Text Editor: Data Structures | Hacker News","url":"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=15381886"},{"title":"Piece table - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piece_table"}]

Here's a comprehensive overview of the comparison between rope data structures, piece tables, and gap buffers for text editors:

## Overview of the Data Structures

The most popular data structures are gap buffers, piece tables, and ropes.

**Gap Buffer**: A gap buffer is just an array that is optimized for inserting at a "cursor" instead of at the end.(no content)The idea is that operations on text are often localized—most of the time, we're not jumping all over the document.

**Piece Table**: A piece table is a data structure typically used to represent a text document while it is edited in a text editor. Initially a reference to the whole of the original file is created, and subsequent inserts and deletes replace a span by combinations of one, two, or three references to sections of either the original document or to a buffer holding inserted text.

**Rope**: A rope is a data structure composed of smaller strings that is used to efficiently store and manipulate longer strings or entire texts. For example, a text editing program may use a rope to represent the text being edited, so that operations such as insertion, deletion, and random access can be done efficiently.

## Performance Characteristics

With ropes, you get worst case O(log n) behavior for all editing operations. In contrast, in benchmarks, the gap buffer is fastest, but not by any meaningful amount, and in the average case, insert and delete performance is fairly comparable between the different data structures.

However, as files get larger the extra latency associated with gap buffers starts to show itself.(no content)For an editor that encourages non-localized edits, ropes are a great choice because they always have good performance, whereas gap buffers degrade poorly with unfavorable editing patterns.

## Real-World Usage

Emacs uses gap buffers, VS Code uses a twist on piece tables, Vim has its own tree data structure, and Helix uses a rope.

## Undo/Redo

Because even deleted text is still included in the piece table, this makes multi-level or unlimited undo easier to implement with a piece table than with alternative data structures such as a gap buffer.

## Concurrency

Both rope implementations (Crop and Ropey) support concurrent access from multiple threads, which lets you take snapshots to do asynchronous saves, backups, or multi-user edits.

I've completed a web search for information about Quill Delta format document model operations. Here's what I found:

## Overview of Delta Format Operations

A Delta is made up of an Array of Operations, which describe changes to a document and can be an insert, delete or retain.

### Insert Operation
Insert operations have an insert key defined, where a String value represents inserting text.(no content)An optional attributes key can be defined with an Object to describe additional formatting information.

### Delete Operation
The delete operation instructs exactly what it implies: delete the next number of characters.

### Retain Operation
A retain operation simply means keep the next number of characters, without modification.(no content)If attributes is specified, it still means keep those characters, but apply the formatting specified by the attributes object.

## Key Characteristics

Operations do not take an index and always describe the change at the current index.(no content)Deltas represents both documents and changes to documents, and if you think of Deltas as the instructions from going from one document to another, the way Deltas represent a document is by expressing the instructions starting from an empty document.

The search results include practical examples and technical documentation from the official Quill documentation and related libraries.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about flat vs. nested document model trade-offs and normalization:

## Key Trade-offs

**Normalization (Flat Models)**
Normalization is a way of defining your database schema in a way that is optimized for fast and high integrity writes by ensuring no redundant data across tables.(no content)Normalized models store data in their most compact form, optimize for writes to the database, and make updates easier since each table is a single source of truth for the information contained within.

**Denormalization (Nested Models)**
Document databases denormalize more aggressively, embedding related data within documents to avoid expensive lookups across collections, which trades storage space and update complexity for read performance.(no content)A denormalized structure can often be easier to read from since all of the data exists in one place, eliminating the need to join multiple tables together.

## Performance Considerations

Normalization prioritizes data integrity while denormalization prioritizes query speed and simplicity, with flattening data able to drastically improve query performance. However, too much normalization leads to queries requiring joins, while too much denormalization leads to large documents that will likely lead to unnecessary data reads from persistent storage and other adverse effects.

## Balanced Approach

It doesn't always have to be either-or, and mixing embedded and referenced data might make sense based on an application's specific usage patterns and workloads.

Part 7: Abstract Syntax Trees - Ruslan's Blog","url":"https://ruslanspivak.com/lsbasi-part7/"},{"title":"Abstract Syntax Tree - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/abstract-syntax-tree"},{"title":"DSL development based on target meta-models.","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/0801.1219"},{"title":"What is an Abstract Syntax Tree? | Nearform","url":"https://nearform.com/insights/what-is-an-abstract-syntax-tree/"},{"title":"Leveling Up One’s Parsing Game With ASTs | by Vaidehi Joshi | basecs | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/basecs/leveling-up-ones-parsing-game-with-asts-d7a6fc2400ff"},{"title":"GitHub - syntax-tree/mdast: Markdown Abstract Syntax Tree format","url":"https://github.com/syntax-tree/mdast"},{"title":"Abstract Syntax Tree in 5 minutes! | by Ray | CodeX | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/codex/abstract-syntax-tree-in-5-minutes-8c174e55ae86"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comparison of text editor document models and Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs):

## Key Differences

**AST Characteristics:**
ASTs don't represent every detail from the real syntax (that's why they're called abstract) - no rule nodes and no parentheses, for example. ASTs are dense compared to a parse tree for the same language construct.

**Document Models vs ASTs:**
In the Eclipse development environment, the Java model is a light-weight and fault tolerant representation of the Java project. It does not contain as many information as the AST but is fast to create. This represents a trade-off: document models prioritize speed and accessibility, while ASTs provide more comprehensive structural information.

## Purpose and Use Cases

The Abstract Syntax Tree maps plain PHP source code in a tree form. This tree is more convenient and reliable to analyze and modify programmatically than text-based source.

Code completion, refactoring, linting and formatting are all powered by the source code tree representation! This is how our IDE and powerful editors implement extraordinary features.

## Complexity Considerations

The level of abstraction that those systems need varies widely. Source-to-source systems, including syntax-directed editors, code-refactoring tools, and automatic parallelization systems, often use an ast with near-source abstractions. The choice of abstraction level directly affects both implementation complexity and functionality.

I found comprehensive information about BlockNote, Tiptap, and their internal data structures. Here's what the search revealed:

## BlockNote Architecture & JSON Serialization

BlockNote's ExportManager handles serialization of the editor's content to various output formats.(no content)These include Full HTML (which preserves all BlockNote-specific attributes and structure for round-tripping back to blocks), Lossy HTML, and Lossy Markdown.(no content)BlockNote uses a manager pattern to separate concerns and organize its internal architecture, where each manager is responsible for a specific domain of functionality.

## Internal Node Structure

The document is stored internally as a ProseMirror node and can be retrieved as a Tiptap JSON object by calling editor.getJSON(), with Tiptap JSON being the recommended format for storing the document.(no content)Tiptap's JSON structure looks like a tree of nodes/marks, with a basic example showing: `{ "type": "doc", "content": [ { "type": "paragraph", "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "Hello World" }] } ] }`

## Schema Definition

Tiptap is based on a schema that defines how content is structured, including the kind of nodes that may occur in the document, their attributes, and the way they can be nested.(no content)BlockNote works with the editor's blockCache (a WeakMap<Node, Block>) to ensure referential equality, guaranteeing that if the underlying ProseMirror Node hasn't changed, the same Block object reference is returned.

content.thinking: Tenho informações suficientes agora. Vou criar um mindmap abrangente com perguntas e respostas para ajudar o usuário a entender profundamente as estruturas de dados usadas em editores de texto. Vou responder em português como solicitado.

content.text:
Excelente pergunta! Vou montar um mindmap textual explorando o tema profundamente. --- # Mindmap: Estruturas de Dados em Editores de Texto Rico ## CATEGORIA 1: Modelo de Documento — Árvore vs Sequência Flat **P: Os blocos são como uma AST?** R: Sim e não. São árvores, mas **muito mais simples** que ASTs de linguagens de programação. Uma AST do TypeScript precisa representar semântica complexa (scopes, tipos, expressões aninhadas). Um documento de editor é basicamente: `Doc → Blocks → Inline Content`. A profundidade típica é 3-5 níveis vs 20+ níveis de uma AST de código. **P: Qual a estrutura mínima viável?** R: **Array linear de operações** (Delta do Quill). Não é árvore: ```json [ { "insert": "Hello ", "attributes": { "bold": true } }, { "insert": "World" } ] ``` Funciona bem para documentos simples. Quebra quando você precisa de tabelas aninhadas. **P: Por que o Slate escolheu árvore aninhada em vez de flat?** R: Porque editores "flat" (como o Quill original) tornavam **tabelas, citações aninhadas e embeds** impossíveis ou muito hackish. A árvore permite `Table → Row → Cell → Paragraph → Text` naturalmente. **P: ProseMirror é árvore, mas o inline é flat. Por quê?** R: Trade-off inteligente. Blocos (paragraphs, headings) formam árvore. Mas **dentro** de um bloco, o texto é sequência flat com "marks" (bold, italic) como metadata anexada. Isso evita a complexidade de `

texto

` virar árvore profunda. **P: Isso significa que marks não são nós?** R: Exato. Em ProseMirror, marks são **anotações em ranges**, não estrutura. `"Hello"` com bold não é `BoldNode → TextNode("Hello")`, é `TextNode("Hello", marks=[bold])`. Simplifica muito as operações. --- ## CATEGORIA 2: Estruturas de Baixo Nível para Texto **P: O que editores como VS Code usam internamente para o buffer de texto?** R: **Piece Table** (variação). Não modifica o texto original — mantém referências a pedaços. Insert/delete cria novos "pieces" apontando para buffers. **P: Quais são as 3 estruturas clássicas?** | Estrutura | Insert/Delete | Random Access | Usado por | |-----------|---------------|---------------|-----------| | **Gap Buffer** | O(n) worst, O(1) local | O(1) | Emacs | | **Piece Table** | O(log n) | O(log n) | VS Code | | **Rope** | O(log n) | O(log n) | Zed, Helix | **P: Por que não usar array simples?** R: Inserir no meio de um array de 1M caracteres = mover 500K caracteres. Gap buffer resolve isso para edições locais. Rope/Piece Table resolvem para edições em qualquer posição. **P: Rope é uma árvore?** R: Sim! É uma **árvore binária balanceada** onde folhas são strings pequenas (64-512 chars). Concatenar duas ropes = criar nó pai. Split = particionar a árvore. Operações são O(log n). **P: Editores rich text (Notion, etc) usam essas estruturas?** R: **Não diretamente**. Gap buffers/ropes são para editores de **código** (texto puro gigante). Editores rich text trabalham no nível de **blocos/nós**, não caracteres individuais. O "texto" dentro de cada bloco geralmente é pequeno o suficiente para arrays simples. --- ## CATEGORIA 3: Imutabilidade e Estado **P: Por que ProseMirror/Lexical tratam documentos como imutáveis?** R: Três razões: 1. **Undo/Redo trivial** — histórico é lista de estados 2. **Diffing eficiente** — comparar referências, não conteúdo 3. **Concorrência** — snapshots não mudam enquanto você processa **P: Isso não é lento? Copiar documento inteiro a cada keystroke?** R: **Structural sharing**. Só os nós modificados são recriados. Se você edita um parágrafo no meio, 99% da árvore mantém as mesmas referências. **P: Lexical congela (freeze) os nós. Por quê?** R: Após reconciliação, `Object.freeze()` recursivo. Isso: - Garante imutabilidade (erros se tentar modificar) - Permite time-travel debugging - Clone só quando necessário via `node.getWritable()` --- ## CATEGORIA 4: Normalização e Constraints **P: O que é "normalização" em editores?** R: Garantir que o documento sempre satisfaz invariantes do schema. Exemplo Slate: - Blocos só contêm outros blocos OU (inlines + text), nunca misturado - Nós de texto vazios são removidos - Textos adjacentes com mesmos marks são fundidos **P: Isso acontece automaticamente?** R: Sim, após cada operação. O editor "conserta" estruturas inválidas. Se você deletar todo conteúdo de um parágrafo, ele insere um TextNode vazio automaticamente. **P: Precisa de schema validado como JSON Schema ou Zod?** R: Depende do editor: - **ProseMirror**: Schema declarativo obrigatório (define nós, content expressions, marks) - **Slate**: Schema-less por padrão, você adiciona normalizadores custom - **Lexical**: Tipos de nós registrados, validação via métodos **P: ProseMirror content expressions são como regex?** R: Sim! `content: "paragraph+"` = um ou mais parágrafos. `content: "(paragraph | heading)*"` = zero ou mais de cada. É uma mini-linguagem de gramática. --- ## CATEGORIA 5: Comparação de Editores Open-Source **P: Qual modelo cada um usa?** | Editor | Modelo | Inline | Schema | |--------|--------|--------|--------| | **ProseMirror** | Árvore de nós | Flat + marks | Declarativo obrigatório | | **Slate** | Árvore aninhada (DOM-like) | Também árvore | Schema-less (normalizers) | | **Lexical** | Árvore de nós tipados | Nós especializados | Registro de tipos | | **Quill** | Delta (array de ops) | N/A (tudo é op) | Implícito nos formats | | **Tiptap** | ProseMirror wrapper | ProseMirror | ProseMirror | | **BlockNote** | ProseMirror + abstração | ProseMirror | JSON blocks | **P: Qual é mais simples de entender?** R: **Quill Delta** é conceitualmente mais simples — documento é literalmente `[op, op, op]`. Mas paga o preço em features avançadas. **P: Qual escala melhor para documentos enormes?** R: **ProseMirror** e **Lexical** têm otimizações sérias. Lexical foi construído para Facebook (milhões de usuários). ProseMirror tem décadas de refinamento. --- ## CATEGORIA 6: Colaboração e CRDTs **P: A estrutura de dados afeta colaboração?** R: **Enormemente**. CRDTs (Yjs, Automerge) precisam de estruturas que suportem merge sem conflitos. **P: Yjs funciona com qualquer modelo?** R: Yjs expõe **tipos compartilhados** (`Y.Text`, `Y.Array`, `Y.Map`). O binding com cada editor mapeia esses tipos para o modelo interno. Funciona com ProseMirror, Slate, Quill, Lexical. **P: Por que texto colaborativo é diferente de JSON colaborativo?** R: Texto precisa de **intenção preservada**. Se eu insiro "A" na posição 5 e você deleta posição 4, meu "A" deve ir para posição 4. CRDTs de texto (como os de Yjs) lidam com isso. CRDTs de JSON genérico não. **P: Peritext resolve o problema de formatação?** R: Sim! Problema: eu aplico bold em "Hello" enquanto você edita "Hello World". Onde o bold termina? [Peritext](https://www.inkandswitch.com/peritext/) é um CRDT especificamente para rich text com marks. --- ## CATEGORIA 7: Trade-offs Fundamentais **P: Árvore profunda vs flat — qual escolher?** | Flat (Delta/ops) | Árvore | |------------------|--------| | ✅ Simples de serializar | ✅ Estruturas complexas (tabelas) | | ✅ Fácil de aplicar transformações | ✅ Traversal intuitivo | | ❌ Não representa aninhamento natural | ❌ Mais complexo de implementar | | ❌ Embeds/tabelas são hacks | ❌ Pode over-engineer | **P: Schema rígido vs flexível?** | Schema rígido (ProseMirror) | Schema-less (Slate) | |-----------------------------|---------------------| | ✅ Documentos sempre válidos | ✅ Prototipar rápido | | ✅ Erros pegos cedo | ✅ Casos de uso imprevistos | | ❌ Setup inicial maior | ❌ Bugs de estrutura em runtime | | ❌ Menos flexível | ❌ Normalizers podem ser complexos | **P: Preciso de estrutura tão complexa quanto TypeScript AST?** R: **Não**. Documentos de texto são ordens de magnitude mais simples. TypeScript AST tem centenas de tipos de nós, scopes, símbolos, tipos inferidos. Um editor rico típico tem ~20-30 tipos de blocos e ~10 marks. --- ## CATEGORIA 8: Unknown Unknowns (Descobertos na Pesquisa) **P: O que é "decorations" e por que importa?** R: Overlays visuais que **não alteram o documento**. Exemplos: highlight de busca, cursor de colaborador, lint warnings. ProseMirror e Slate suportam nativamente. Crucial para features que parecem simples mas são armadilhas. **P: Por que VS Code usa piece table e não rope?** R: Piece tables têm [undo/redo built-in grátis](https://www.averylaird.com/programming/the%20text%20editor/2017/09/30/the-piece-table) — texto deletado ainda existe no buffer original. Ropes precisam de mecanismo separado de histórico. **P: BlockNote abstrai ProseMirror. Qual o custo?** R: Usa [WeakMap como cache de blocos](https://deepwiki.com/TypeCellOS/BlockNote/2.2-prosemirror-and-tiptap-integration). Se o nó ProseMirror não mudou, retorna mesma referência. Overhead mínimo, mas você perde acesso direto às primitivas ProseMirror quando precisa. **P: Por que Notion não usa framework open-source?** R: Escala e requisitos específicos. Notion implementa própria [fila de renderização e transactions](https://yuexun.me/how-the-notion-editor-works/). Integra com backend proprietário, real-time sync, permissions granulares. Frameworks genéricos não atendem. **P: Formatação é armazenada como HTML?** R: **Quase nunca**. Notion, ProseMirror, Slate — todos usam JSON estruturado. HTML é **output** de renderização, não storage. Isso permite múltiplos renderers (web, mobile, PDF) do mesmo dado. --- ## Resumo Visual ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ DOCUMENTO │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Alto nível: Blocos/Nós (árvore simples) │ │ ┌─────────┐ │ │ │ Doc │ │ │ └────┬────┘ │ │ ├── Paragraph ── [TextNode + marks] │ │ ├── Heading ──── [TextNode + marks] │ │ └── Table ────── Row ── Cell ── Paragraph │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Baixo nível: Buffer de texto (só editores de código) │ │ Gap Buffer | Piece Table | Rope │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Colaboração: CRDT layer │ │ Yjs Y.Text | Automerge | Peritext │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- **Sources:** - [ProseMirror Guide](https://prosemirror.net/docs/guide/) - [Slate Docs - Normalizing](https://docs.slatejs.org/concepts/11-normalizing) - [Lexical Introduction](https://lexical.dev/docs/intro) - [Quill Delta Format](https://quilljs.com/docs/delta/) - [Gap Buffers vs Ropes](https://coredumped.dev/2023/08/09/text-showdown-gap-buffers-vs-ropes/) - [Piece Table](https://www.averylaird.com/programming/the%20text%20editor/2017/09/30/the-piece-table) - [Zed's Rope & SumTree](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-rope-sumtree) - [How Notion Editor Works](https://yuexun.me/how-the-notion-editor-works/) - [Notion Data Model](https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion) - [Yjs Docs](https://docs.yjs.dev/) - [Peritext CRDT](https://www.inkandswitch.com/peritext/) - [BlockNote Internals](https://deepwiki.com/TypeCellOS/BlockNote/2.2-prosemirror-and-tiptap-integration) - [Tiptap Schema](https://tiptap.dev/docs/editor/core-concepts/schema)

input.content:
# Block Renderer Research: Data Structures in Rich Text Editors > Discovery document 001 - Estruturas de dados fundamentais --- ## Overview Este documento explora as estruturas de dados usadas internamente por editores de texto rico como Notion, ProseMirror, Slate, Lexical e outros. Foco em modelos de documento, trade-offs e decisões arquiteturais. --- ## 1. Modelo de Documento — Árvore vs Sequência Flat ### Os blocos são como uma AST? Sim e não. São árvores, mas **muito mais simples** que ASTs de linguagens de programação. Uma AST do TypeScript precisa representar semântica complexa (scopes, tipos, expressões aninhadas). Um documento de editor é basicamente: `Doc → Blocks → Inline Content`. A profundidade típica é 3-5 níveis vs 20+ níveis de uma AST de código. ### Estrutura mínima viável **Array linear de operações** (Delta do Quill). Não é árvore: ```json [ { "insert": "Hello ", "attributes": { "bold": true } }, { "insert": "World" } ] ``` Funciona bem para documentos simples. Quebra quando você precisa de tabelas aninhadas. ### Por que Slate escolheu árvore aninhada? Porque editores "flat" (como o Quill original) tornavam **tabelas, citações aninhadas e embeds** impossíveis ou muito hackish. A árvore permite `Table → Row → Cell → Paragraph → Text` naturalmente. ### ProseMirror: árvore para blocos, flat para inline Trade-off inteligente. Blocos (paragraphs, headings) formam árvore. Mas **dentro** de um bloco, o texto é sequência flat com "marks" (bold, italic) como metadata anexada. Isso evita a complexidade de `

texto

` virar árvore profunda. ### Marks não são nós Em ProseMirror, marks são **anotações em ranges**, não estrutura. `"Hello"` com bold não é `BoldNode → TextNode("Hello")`, é `TextNode("Hello", marks=[bold])`. Simplifica muito as operações. --- ## 2. Estruturas de Baixo Nível para Texto ### Estruturas clássicas para buffers de texto | Estrutura | Insert/Delete | Random Access | Usado por | |-----------|---------------|---------------|-----------| | **Gap Buffer** | O(n) worst, O(1) local | O(1) | Emacs | | **Piece Table** | O(log n) | O(log n) | VS Code | | **Rope** | O(log n) | O(log n) | Zed, Helix | ### Por que não array simples? Inserir no meio de um array de 1M caracteres = mover 500K caracteres. Gap buffer resolve isso para edições locais. Rope/Piece Table resolvem para edições em qualquer posição. ### Rope é uma árvore Sim! É uma **árvore binária balanceada** onde folhas são strings pequenas (64-512 chars). Concatenar duas ropes = criar nó pai. Split = particionar a árvore. Operações são O(log n). ### Editores rich text usam essas estruturas? **Não diretamente**. Gap buffers/ropes são para editores de **código** (texto puro gigante). Editores rich text trabalham no nível de **blocos/nós**, não caracteres individuais. O "texto" dentro de cada bloco geralmente é pequeno o suficiente para arrays simples. --- ## 3. Imutabilidade e Estado ### Por que ProseMirror/Lexical tratam documentos como imutáveis? 1. **Undo/Redo trivial** — histórico é lista de estados 2. **Diffing eficiente** — comparar referências, não conteúdo 3. **Concorrência** — snapshots não mudam enquanto você processa ### Structural sharing Não copia documento inteiro a cada keystroke. Só os nós modificados são recriados. Se você edita um parágrafo no meio, 99% da árvore mantém as mesmas referências. ### Lexical freeze Após reconciliação, `Object.freeze()` recursivo: - Garante imutabilidade (erros se tentar modificar) - Permite time-travel debugging - Clone só quando necessário via `node.getWritable()` --- ## 4. Normalização e Constraints ### O que é normalização? Garantir que o documento sempre satisfaz invariantes do schema. Exemplo Slate: - Blocos só contêm outros blocos OU (inlines + text), nunca misturado - Nós de texto vazios são removidos - Textos adjacentes com mesmos marks são fundidos ### Schema em cada editor | Editor | Abordagem | |--------|-----------| | **ProseMirror** | Schema declarativo obrigatório | | **Slate** | Schema-less, normalizers custom | | **Lexical** | Tipos de nós registrados | ### ProseMirror content expressions Como regex! `content: "paragraph+"` = um ou mais parágrafos. `content: "(paragraph | heading)*"` = zero ou mais de cada. --- ## 5. Comparação de Editores Open-Source | Editor | Modelo | Inline | Schema | |--------|--------|--------|--------| | **ProseMirror** | Árvore de nós | Flat + marks | Declarativo obrigatório | | **Slate** | Árvore aninhada (DOM-like) | Também árvore | Schema-less (normalizers) | | **Lexical** | Árvore de nós tipados | Nós especializados | Registro de tipos | | **Quill** | Delta (array de ops) | N/A (tudo é op) | Implícito nos formats | | **Tiptap** | ProseMirror wrapper | ProseMirror | ProseMirror | | **BlockNote** | ProseMirror + abstração | ProseMirror | JSON blocks | ### Simplicidade vs Features **Quill Delta** é conceitualmente mais simples — documento é literalmente `[op, op, op]`. Mas paga o preço em features avançadas. ### Escala **ProseMirror** e **Lexical** têm otimizações sérias. Lexical foi construído para Facebook (milhões de usuários). --- ## 6. Colaboração e CRDTs ### Estrutura afeta colaboração **Enormemente**. CRDTs (Yjs, Automerge) precisam de estruturas que suportem merge sem conflitos. ### Yjs shared types Expõe `Y.Text`, `Y.Array`, `Y.Map`. Bindings mapeiam para modelo interno de cada editor. Funciona com ProseMirror, Slate, Quill, Lexical. ### Peritext Problema: eu aplico bold em "Hello" enquanto você edita "Hello World". Onde o bold termina? Peritext é um CRDT especificamente para rich text com marks. --- ## 7. Trade-offs Fundamentais ### Árvore profunda vs flat | Flat (Delta/ops) | Árvore | |------------------|--------| | Simples de serializar | Estruturas complexas (tabelas) | | Fácil de aplicar transformações | Traversal intuitivo | | Não representa aninhamento natural | Mais complexo de implementar | | Embeds/tabelas são hacks | Pode over-engineer | ### Schema rígido vs flexível | Schema rígido (ProseMirror) | Schema-less (Slate) | |-----------------------------|---------------------| | Documentos sempre válidos | Prototipar rápido | | Erros pegos cedo | Casos de uso imprevistos | | Setup inicial maior | Bugs de estrutura em runtime | | Menos flexível | Normalizers podem ser complexos | ### Complexidade vs TypeScript AST **Não precisa ser tão complexo**. Documentos de texto são ordens de magnitude mais simples. TypeScript AST tem centenas de tipos de nós, scopes, símbolos, tipos inferidos. Um editor rico típico tem ~20-30 tipos de blocos e ~10 marks. --- ## 8. Insights Importantes ### Decorations Overlays visuais que **não alteram o documento**. Exemplos: highlight de busca, cursor de colaborador, lint warnings. Crucial para features que parecem simples mas são armadilhas. ### VS Code usa piece table, não rope Piece tables têm undo/redo built-in grátis — texto deletado ainda existe no buffer original. ### BlockNote abstrai ProseMirror Usa WeakMap como cache de blocos. Se o nó ProseMirror não mudou, retorna mesma referência. ### Notion não usa framework open-source Escala e requisitos específicos. Implementa própria fila de renderização e transactions. Integra com backend proprietário. ### Formatação não é HTML Quase nunca. Notion, ProseMirror, Slate — todos usam JSON estruturado. HTML é **output** de renderização, não storage. --- ## Resumo Visual ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ DOCUMENTO │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Alto nível: Blocos/Nós (árvore simples) │ │ ┌─────────┐ │ │ │ Doc │ │ │ └────┬────┘ │ │ ├── Paragraph ── [TextNode + marks] │ │ ├── Heading ──── [TextNode + marks] │ │ └── Table ────── Row ── Cell ── Paragraph │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Baixo nível: Buffer de texto (só editores de código) │ │ Gap Buffer | Piece Table | Rope │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Colaboração: CRDT layer │ │ Yjs Y.Text | Automerge | Peritext │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- ## References - [ProseMirror Guide](https://prosemirror.net/docs/guide/) - [Slate Docs - Normalizing](https://docs.slatejs.org/concepts/11-normalizing) - [Lexical Introduction](https://lexical.dev/docs/intro) - [Quill Delta Format](https://quilljs.com/docs/delta/) - [Gap Buffers vs Ropes](https://coredumped.dev/2023/08/09/text-showdown-gap-buffers-vs-ropes/) - [Piece Table](https://www.averylaird.com/programming/the%20text%20editor/2017/09/30/the-piece-table) - [Zed's Rope & SumTree](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-rope-sumtree) - [How Notion Editor Works](https://yuexun.me/how-the-notion-editor-works/) - [Notion Data Model](https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion) - [Yjs Docs](https://docs.yjs.dev/) - [Peritext CRDT](https://www.inkandswitch.com/peritext/) - [BlockNote Internals](https://deepwiki.com/TypeCellOS/BlockNote/2.2-prosemirror-and-tiptap-integration) - [Tiptap Schema](https://tiptap.dev/docs/editor/core-concepts/schema) --- *Document created: 2026-02-12*

Se você edita um parágrafo no meio, 99% da árvore mantém as mesmas referências. ### Lexical freeze Após reconciliação, `Object.freeze()` recursivo: - Garante imutabilidade (erros se tentar modificar) - Permite time-travel debugging - Clone só quando necessário via `node.getWritable()` --- ## 4. Normalização e Constraints ### O que é normalização? Garantir que o documento sempre satisfaz invariantes do schema. Exemplo Slate: - Blocos só contêm outros blocos OU (inlines + text), nunca misturado - Nós de texto vazios são removidos - Textos adjacentes com mesmos marks são fundidos ### Schema em cada editor | Editor | Abordagem | |--------|-----------| | **ProseMirror** | Schema declarativo obrigatório | | **Slate** | Schema-less, normalizers custom | | **Lexical** | Tipos de nós registrados | ### ProseMirror content expressions Como regex! `content: "paragraph+"` = um ou mais parágrafos. `content: "(paragraph | heading)*"` = zero ou mais de cada. --- ## 5. Comparação de Editores Open-Source | Editor | Modelo | Inline | Schema | |--------|--------|--------|--------| | **ProseMirror** | Árvore de nós | Flat + marks | Declarativo obrigatório | | **Slate** | Árvore aninhada (DOM-like) | Também árvore | Schema-less (normalizers) | | **Lexical** | Árvore de nós tipados | Nós especializados | Registro de tipos | | **Quill** | Delta (array de ops) | N/A (tudo é op) | Implícito nos formats | | **Tiptap** | ProseMirror wrapper | ProseMirror | ProseMirror | | **BlockNote** | ProseMirror + abstração | ProseMirror | JSON blocks | ### Simplicidade vs Features **Quill Delta** é conceitualmente mais simples — documento é literalmente `[op, op, op]`. Mas paga o preço em features avançadas. ### Escala **ProseMirror** e **Lexical** têm otimizações sérias. Lexical foi construído para Facebook (milhões de usuários). --- ## 6. Colaboração e CRDTs ### Estrutura afeta colaboração **Enormemente**. CRDTs (Yjs, Automerge) precisam de estruturas que suportem merge sem conflitos. ### Yjs shared types Expõe `Y.Text`, `Y.Array`, `Y.Map`. Bindings mapeiam para modelo interno de cada editor. Funciona com ProseMirror, Slate, Quill, Lexical. ### Peritext Problema: eu aplico bold em "Hello" enquanto você edita "Hello World". Onde o bold termina? Peritext é um CRDT especificamente para rich text com marks. --- ## 7. Trade-offs Fundamentais ### Árvore profunda vs flat | Flat (Delta/ops) | Árvore | |------------------|--------| | Simples de serializar | Estruturas complexas (tabelas) | | Fácil de aplicar transformações | Traversal intuitivo | | Não representa aninhamento natural | Mais complexo de implementar | | Embeds/tabelas são hacks | Pode over-engineer | ### Schema rígido vs flexível | Schema rígido (ProseMirror) | Schema-less (Slate) | |-----------------------------|---------------------| | Documentos sempre válidos | Prototipar rápido | | Erros pegos cedo | Casos de uso imprevistos | | Setup inicial maior | Bugs de estrutura em runtime | | Menos flexível | Normalizers podem ser complexos | ### Complexidade vs TypeScript AST **Não precisa ser tão complexo**. Documentos de texto são ordens de magnitude mais simples. TypeScript AST tem centenas de tipos de nós, scopes, símbolos, tipos inferidos. Um editor rico típico tem ~20-30 tipos de blocos e ~10 marks. --- ## 8. Insights Importantes ### Decorations Overlays visuais que **não alteram o documento**. Exemplos: highlight de busca, cursor de colaborador, lint warnings. Crucial para features que parecem simples mas são armadilhas. ### VS Code usa piece table, não rope Piece tables têm undo/redo built-in grátis — texto deletado ainda existe no buffer original. ### BlockNote abstrai ProseMirror Usa WeakMap como cache de blocos. Se o nó ProseMirror não mudou, retorna mesma referência. ### Notion não usa framework open-source Escala e requisitos específicos. Implementa própria fila de renderização e transactions. Integra com backend proprietário. ### Formatação não é HTML Quase nunca. Notion, ProseMirror, Slate — todos usam JSON estruturado. HTML é **output** de renderização, não storage. --- ## Resumo Visual ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ DOCUMENTO │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Alto nível: Blocos/Nós (árvore simples) │ │ ┌─────────┐ │ │ │ Doc │ │ │ └────┬────┘ │ │ ├── Paragraph ── [TextNode + marks] │ │ ├── Heading ──── [TextNode + marks] │ │ └── Table ────── Row ── Cell ── Paragraph │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Baixo nível: Buffer de texto (só editores de código) │ │ Gap Buffer | Piece Table | Rope │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Colaboração: CRDT layer │ │ Yjs Y.Text | Automerge | Peritext │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- ## References - [ProseMirror Guide](https://prosemirror.net/docs/guide/) - [Slate Docs - Normalizing](https://docs.slatejs.org/concepts/11-normalizing) - [Lexical Introduction](https://lexical.dev/docs/intro) - [Quill Delta Format](https://quilljs.com/docs/delta/) - [Gap Buffers vs Ropes](https://coredumped.dev/2023/08/09/text-showdown-gap-buffers-vs-ropes/) - [Piece Table](https://www.averylaird.com/programming/the%20text%20editor/2017/09/30/the-piece-table) - [Zed's Rope & SumTree](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-rope-sumtree) - [How Notion Editor Works](https://yuexun.me/how-the-notion-editor-works/) - [Notion Data Model](https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion) - [Yjs Docs](https://docs.yjs.dev/) - [Peritext CRDT](https://www.inkandswitch.com/peritext/) - [BlockNote Internals](https://deepwiki.com/TypeCellOS/BlockNote/2.2-prosemirror-and-tiptap-integration) - [Tiptap Schema](https://tiptap.dev/docs/editor/core-concepts/schema) --- *Document created: 2026-02-12*

Here's what the search results reveal about PDF internal structure, document object model, and content streams:

## PDF Components

A PDF is composed of four components: objects, file structure, document structure and content streams.(no content)These components include the header (which identifies the PDF version number and binary nature), the body (which contains all document objects including pages, fonts, images, and graphical content), and the cross-reference table (which provides precise byte-offset mapping for random object access).

## Document Object Model

The root of the PDF document object model is the Document Catalog dictionary.(no content)At the heart of PDF manipulation lies the document object model (DOM), a hierarchical structure that organizes all PDF components.(no content)Document Object Model (DOM) is a form of representation of structured documents as an object-oriented model. DOM is the official World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) standard for representing structured documents in a platform and language-neutral manner. In simple words, DOM is a tree of objects that represent the structure of some document.

## Basic Object Types

There are nine types of objects: null, Boolean, integer, real, name, string, array, dictionary, and stream.

## Content Streams

Streams are used for content streams (drawing commands), images, fonts, ICC color profiles, metadata, and more.(no content)Content Streams is a stream that has instructions on how to display text & graphics on the corresponding page.(no content)Each page contains a content stream that stores drawing operations as a sequence of PDF operators.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of UI layout tree data structures in Flutter and the relationship between render trees and widget trees:

## Flutter's Three-Tree Architecture

Flutter builds your UI using three invisible trees: the Widget Tree (the blueprint of what you want to show), the Element Tree (the real objects in memory), and the Render Tree (the paint instructions for what's drawn on the screen).

### Widget Tree
The Widget Tree is a declarative structure of your app's user interface where widgets define the configuration for UI elements, such as text, buttons, and layouts. Widgets are immutable and don't change once created, just like a paper blueprint doesn't magically adjust if someone moves a chair inside the house.

### Element Tree
After being built, the widgets are held by the element tree, which retains the logical structure of the user interface, and the element tree is necessary because the widgets themselves are immutable, which means they cannot remember their parent or child relationships with other widgets.

### Render Tree vs Widget Tree
The render tree is a data structure that stores the geometry of the user interface, which is computed during layout and used during painting and hit testing. The key difference is that the Render Tree is responsible for the layout and painting of widgets and represents how widgets will appear on the screen while ensuring efficient rendering.

Not all widgets have render objects; some, like StatelessWidget or StatefulWidget, are purely structural.

## Performance Optimizations

Flutter's widget building algorithm is sublinear, and the framework keeps a list of dirty elements and jumps directly to them during the build phase, skipping over clean elements.

I've completed the web search on browser rendering engines and the various stages of the rendering pipeline. Here are the key findings:

## Browser Rendering Pipeline Overview

The Critical Rendering Path is the sequence of steps the browser goes through to convert the HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen.

### The Rendering Stages

The critical rendering path includes the Document Object Model (DOM), CSS Object Model (CSSOM), render tree and layout. Here's how each component works:

**DOM and CSSOM Construction:**
- The document object model is created as the HTML is parsed.
- The HTML includes or makes requests for styles, which in turn builds the CSS object model.

**Render Tree:**
The browser combines the DOM and CSSOM into a "render tree," which captures all the visible DOM content on the page and all the CSSOM style information for each node.(no content)Render tree contains only the nodes required to render the page.

**Layout (also called Reflow):**
Layout determines the size and location of everything on the page.(no content)Computed styles are used to compute each element's geometry creating a layout tree.

**Paint and Composite:**
Rendering steps include style, layout, paint, and in some cases compositing.(no content)The browser may need to composite layers (especially when animations, position changes, or z-indexes are involved), before displaying them.

The browser combines the DOM and CSSOM into a "render tree," which captures all the visible DOM content on the page and all the CSSOM style information for each node.(no content)Render tree contains only the nodes required to render the page.

**Layout (also called Reflow):**
Layout determines the size and location of everything on the page.(no content)Computed styles are used to compute each element's geometry creating a layout tree.

**Paint and Composite:**
Rendering steps include style, layout, paint, and in some cases compositing.(no content)The browser may need to composite layers (especially when animations, position changes, or z-indexes are involved), before displaying them.
